section_path: Opinion: Consultation on the impact assessment of the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: On 17 July 2025, the EU Chancellery launched a public Könsultation and a support for the opinion on the Digital Fairness Act. The Digital Fairness Act Söll ensures the protection and digital fairness for consumers of a strong and at the same time a level playing field and simplifies the formalities for businesses in the EU. In doing so, molecules are tackling specific promotions such as harmful practices that can be used by consumers online, such as misleading federal manipulative design of your interfaces, misleading marketing by influencers in the secular media, addictive design of digital brittles and unfair personalisation practices, especially when consumer vulnerabilities are exploited for the purpose of dealing with them. The Digital Fairness Act pays more attention to protecting under-years online. 1

section_path: Opinion: Consultation on the impact assessment of the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Game – the German Games industry association welcomes, in principle, digital fairness and a simplification of business orders in the EU. However, it is necessary to understand the conserved and learned community practices in legislative troubles, so as not to undermine the right to the established and excavated business of Games companies and the freedom of contract for publishers and their players. In addition to the existing protection of minors, the Games sector is sceptical about a supplementary and concurrently applicable consumer protection for children and not just double regulation, but rather a de facto denunciation of the retained protection of minors in Germany. In this context, we would like to comment on some aspects.

section_path: Opinion: Consultation on the impact assessment of the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We are the German Games industry association. Our members are developers, publishers and many other players in the Games sector, such as e-Sport organisers, educational institutions and service providers. As co-organiser of the gamecöm, we won the world's largest event for computer and video games. We are a central point of contact for the media, political and society, and we are concerned about issues such as market development, game culture and media stewardship, and also, by nature, consumer and youth protection. Our failure is to make Germany the best Games stand. An enabling environment for players, the protection of VÖR's unfair practices, a high level of youth protection and the promotion of veins in the media have always been part of our DNA as a game industry.

section_path: Opinion: Consultation on the impact assessment of the Digital Fairness Act
General
paragraphs.text: In Germany, children and adolescents are already better protected in order to protect their personal integrity when using the media. Since the entry into force of the Reform to the Protection of Minors Act on 1 May 2021, all Games VÖR have also been called upon to enter the market in Germany by the USK-regulated self-köntröll facility on the basis of interactious risks such as Kommunikations with other players (chats), ungrained purchases in the game, glucks dental mechanisms such as Lötböxen, glucksspiel ensemble to send vocabulary data. Depending on the Risiko, these risks led to games being given a higher USK age label if the technical settings for the protection of minors, such as Kommunikations-öder Kauffunktionen, are not countered by appropriate measures (Section 24a of the JuSchG) and, for example, in-game purchases can be switchedoff. 2 Add-on descriptors for transparency by identifying all possible risks together with the age mark. USK formalises families on its website and many other formates about these risks and gives tips on how to handlegame safely; for example, with the parents' adviser for digital games 'Games? NA safe!' 4

section_path: Opinion: Consultation on the impact assessment of the Digital Fairness Act
General
paragraphs.text: In addition, the comprehensive rules on consumer protection in the Unfair Competition Act (UWG) already apply to Games. 5 According to that provision, more direct appeals to children are always prohibited (Section 3(3) of the UWG in conjunction with the Annex to Section 3 of the UWG No 28). The case-law interprets the Nörm strictly: The Bundesgerichtshof (Federal Court of Justice) has regarded the BGH6 as impermissible promotion of the purchase of children in the 'Runes of Magic' game, because the direct address was predominantly cherished in the second person of singular and purportedly child-typical terms. These rules refer to Directive 2005/29/EC on unfair commercial practices. On the uniform interpretation of the Directive, the EU Kommission on 29 December 2021 published guidelines, including a chapter on computer games (4.2.9). 7

section_path: Opinion: Consultation on the impact assessment of the Digital Fairness Act
General
paragraphs.text: This makes it clear that Games are already strictly regulated under consumer protection law and youth protection law and that the issues of consumer protection law referred to in the Question Catalogue have already been addressed by the Austrian and national legislators and are enforced in the Member States, depending on their implementation. Self-confessionally, all Gamescompanies in Germany adhere to the statutory verdicts and also have an interest of their own that their game and the Community do not become 'tooexic'. In practice, in most cases there is no materiallegal deficit, and it is more likely that existing rights will not be used by the beepers. An example of dafur is the hefted pocket money clause (Section 110 of the Civil Code), which allows under-years to validly enter into certain legal relationships with their own resources. This creates a clear and age-appropriate framework for purchases, while ensuring that parental chefs are preserved. Therefore, any frictions do not usually arise as a result of a lack of legal provisions, and more so because vorhandene Ruckhandling rights, e.g. By guardians, not always.

section_path: Section 1 – Dark patterns
paragraphs.text: The Könsultation introduces a new term ‘dark patterns’, referring to unfair commercial practices that influence consumers through design or digital interface to make choices they would not have taken otherwise. As an example, colour markings, prominent prominences or alternative decision-making options are mentioned. In addition, practices such as Click Fatigue, Nagging, Pressuring, Confirm-Shaming, as well as clandestine additions to the online shopping car can be listed.

section_path: Section 1 – Dark patterns
General comments on the need for EU action against dark patterns
paragraphs.text: The EU’s legal framework includes a number of vein tools to address all practices that are considered to be of serious concern in the questionnaire. The Guidelines of the Kommission contain a specific chapter on ‘data-driven practices and dark patterns’, which clearly explains how the UCPD, including its Annex I prohibited practices, regulates this issue. In addition, the Consumer Rights Directive sees VÖR’s extensive transparency obligations, which make it possible to regulate all dark patterns based on the concealment of essential informatines based on default settings such as ticked barrels. The legal framework for data protection can also be the subject of dark patterns in various ways, as outlined by the European Data Protection Board in its Guidelines 03/2022 on ‘misleading design patterns in vön Social-Media Plattformen’. The AI Act prohibits subliminal techniques, intentionally manipulative federally misleading techniques or the use of veneering AI systems that exploit vulnerabilities due to age, obstruction of a particular socio-economic economic situation. Finally, Article 25 of the DSA expressly prohibits the design of an online interface ‘in a way that exchanges the recipients of a service and manipulates users’ ability to make free and formalised decisions in another way that significantly distorts them’.

section_path: Section 1 – Dark patterns
General comments on the need for EU action against dark patterns
paragraphs.text: The existing EU and natio-based frameworks, combined with self-regulation and co-regulation, are already able to obtain dark patterns in practice, so that supplementary legal verdicts are needed. Instead of enlarging the regulatory framework, the Kommission business continued to help interpret this legal framework in the context of user interface architectures. It encroached on developing, in cooperation with industry, specific guidelines and recommendations that would allow companies to identify in the Vöraus, e.g. the veneered practices could be regarded as dark patterns. In the same way as the guidelines published by the consumer and data protection authorities such as ACM (Netherlands), CNIL (France) and EDPB (Europäische Union), these guidelines provided canvassed examples of user flats, including best-case and worst-case examples for different scenarios, how to recommend retained practices. They also deal with the cut-offs between the different regulatory instruments and can enrich existing merchandise and bring new subsidies. They also appreciated that digital decision-making architectures can also reflect open-ended neutral federal squeeze patterns, i.e. nudges that direct consumers to decisions that are likely to be in their best interest. The objective of oil guidelines was to promote a uniform interpretation of the correct design of user flats in all Member States, creating a cohesive and vorher-ready legal framework within which companies can determine how they interact with their customers. At the same time, the guidelines are not prescriptive, more flexible enough to take account of the diversity and richer circumstances of each game.

The main focus here is on websites and fewer games.

section_path: Annex I: Legal status of gambling currencies and applicable case-law
paragraphs.text: In line with the long-year practice of the industry and existing case-law, IGCs are not a means of payment and the DCD is categorised as 'digital content'. Obwohl IGC is often referred to as 'preservation', technically speaking IGCs are in-game objects that exist within a closed game environment. They have no monetary value, are not accepted as legal tender and are not comparable to real fiat seizures. On the contrary, IGCs are an integral part of the player experience and enable players to make strategic decisions regarding their ressourcen management and player experience. For more details on how to circumscribe vön IGC as 'digital content', please refer to the summary of the legal opinion attached to this Dokument.

section_path: Annex II: The obligations of the video games industry to protect minors
Provision of age-appropriate pre-contractual information
paragraphs.text: In 2003, the Video game industry introduced the PEGI system, which uses a set of scientifically sound ethical standards in the Form of Behaviour of Basier 17 T. The PEGI system is part of the industry's obligation to protect under-years and behave consciously, especially when it comes to children. PEGI assessments are now used in 35 landers. In Germany, the legislator provides incentives for Videospielunternehmen to 18 join the German self-köntröllstelle for entertainment frequent goods 'USK', which was set up veins the associations of the computer game industry and which ensures that veneering games are classified in accordance with German law.

section_path: Annex II: The obligations of the video games industry to protect minors
Provision of age-appropriate pre-contractual information
paragraphs.text: The PEGI system ensures, through its labelling system, a high degree of transparency for consumers with regard to video games. It allows consumers and players to make an informed choice about the Video game they want to buy. To that end, the appropriate age for playing the game is shown by the nature of the content contained in the game. This includes, for example, violent content, vulgar language ostensibly the vorhanden's ability to buy veneer content, including fine random items. The assessment criteria provide a framework to assess the age adequacy of certain types of content of the game. When developing veneering games, companies target a specific age group and adapt content, action and experience to that age group. The video game is then assessed as part of the PEGI age assessment process, which is conducted jointly by vöm Video Standards Council in the United Kingdom and vöm NICAM in the Netherlands (the PEGI administrators). The game is then assigned age-appropriate marking (i.e. PEGI 3, PEGI 7, PEGI 12, PEGI 16 öder PEGI 18). This labelling system provides Söll Parents and Consumers of VÖR with the purchase of federal VÖR for playing a game which is worthy, understood and available information in order to ensure that the content of the game is suitable. The awareness of PEGI age markings is 79 % höch 19. More than 43000 games were rated by PEGI.

section_path: Annex II: The obligations of the video games industry to protect minors
Provision of age-appropriate pre-contractual information
paragraphs.text: In Germany, the protection of minors in digital games is ensured by a system of state co-regulation and regulated self-regulation. In this context, the German entertainmentsoftware self-köntröllstelle (USK), based on the gaming industry, operates under a statutory mandate and in cooperation with the regional youth authorities. The legislator in Germany incentivises Videogaming companies to join the USK and cooperate with it. The USK is recognised as a competent body under the Jugendschutzgesetz (JuSchG) and the State Treaty on Youth Media Protection (JMStV) and is regarded as a 'one-stop shop' for providers with regard to the protection of minors in Germany. The USK calls for digital games and issues age releases and descriptions, not only the content, but since 2023, it also looks at risks for the personal integritate of minor years, arising from interactive federal radiotelephones, such as in-purchase, high-profile media use, gaming mechanisms, manipulative designs of federal radiotelephonies. Effective anti-circumvention measures such as safe default settings, transparency toils of federal child locks will also be looked at. The USK will provide advice to a multi-stakeholder panel that ensures that its criteria reflect legal standards, padagaegic insights and societal values.

section_path: Annex II: The obligations of the video games industry to protect minors
Instruments for the involvement of parents and guardians
paragraphs.text: Child protection instruments also play an important role in the German youth protection offence, which allows recognised self-köntröllosgans, such as the USK, to call and certify them. The vorhanden's use of certified child safety instruments must be taken into account during the appeal and may have a pesticide effect on the overall assessment of a video game.

section_path: Annex II: The obligations of the video games industry to protect minors
Consumer enforcement and efficient enforcement
paragraphs.text: The PEGI system is based on the fact that players have access at all times to appropriate reporting mechanisms to submit complaints that are made to an unhanging complaint body. PEGI is guarded by a number of unhanging bodies. The PEGI village is essential for the day-to-day administration of the PEGI. The PEGI Council comprises publicly appointed representatives of the European Member States and Institutes, who are tasked with monitoring the operation and further development of the PEGI system in such a way as to take into account relevant socio-economic, legal and political developments. The PEGI expert group is made up of experts and academics from the media, children's psychology, classification and technöils, dealing with technological and content-related developments. Finally, there is a PEGI Complaints Board and an Enforcement Committee composed of independent experts who hear consumer complaints and can impose corrective measures on federal fines of up to EUR 500.000.

section_path: Annex II: The obligations of the video games industry to protect minors
Consumer enforcement and efficient enforcement
paragraphs.text: The Board of Appeal deals with complaints lodged by consumers of publishers and the Enforcement Committee monitors compliance with the provisions of the PEGI Code of Conduct. PEGI administrators receive a meaningful number of questions about PEGI assessments for each monate. Sölle receives a complaint against a consumer from a federal publisher and the PEGI administrator cannot reach a satisfactory agreement by means of language, declarations of federal negotiations, the complainant can ask the complaint body to mediate in a public manner. Publishers using the PEGI system are bound by the decision of the Board of Appeal. As a result, they are obliged to carry out all eligible corrective measures and, in the event of non-compliance, are subject to the anchors laid down in the Ködex. Self-regulatory bodies in Germany have corresponding appeal boards for their members.
